content: Evidence-based diabetes prevention approaches: (1) Lifestyle programs—CDC-recognized National DPP delivering year-long curriculum on nutrition, physical activity, stress management, problem-solving; delivered in-person, online, or distance learning; (2) Pharmacotherapy—metformin for high-risk individuals (BMI≥35, age<60, history of gestational diabetes), GLP-1 agonists showing promise for weight loss/diabetes prevention; (3) Policy interventions—sugar-sweetened beverage taxes, healthy school food standards, built environment for active living, food labeling requirements; (4) Healthcare system integration—routine prediabetes screening, electronic health record prompts, referral systems to DPP, insurance coverage for prevention programs; (5) Community approaches—faith-based programs, community health worker delivery, culturally adapted interventions; (6) Digital health—mobile apps, remote coaching, wearable devices. Cost-effectiveness analyses show lifestyle intervention cost-effective long-term. Scalability remains challenge requiring innovative delivery models.
